paper_title: Jordan: The Elements of a Growth Strategy
research_answer: The evidence points to genuine structural improvement, not just a commodity price windfall. Jordan's exports grew by a factor of 3.6 between 2000 and 2008 at a CAGR of 17.3% in nominal terms and 14.1% in constant dollars — the real-dollar figure indicating growth well beyond price effects. More importantly, this growth involved significant market share gains in garments, chemicals, and agricultural goods, meaning Jordan was capturing a larger slice of global demand in these categories, not merely benefiting from price tailwinds. That said, the broader regional context — fueled by the Gulf oil boom — did play a role, including through FDI flows from Gulf countries that averaged $2.1 billion per year (12.9% of GDP) from 2003–2009. The subsequent stagnation in market share after 2008, even before the post-2014 regional crisis, suggests the competitiveness gains of the boom era were not deeply entrenched or self-sustaining.
supporting_quotes: This went beyond a mere commodity boom: it entailed significant gains in market share for Jordan's exports, in particular, for garments, chemicals, and agricultural goods.
From 2000 to 2008, Jordan's exports grew by a factor of 3.6, expanding at a CAGR of 17.3% per annum (14.1% in constant dollars).
Between 2008 and 2014, export growth decelerated (3.4% per year, 1.8% in constant dollars), as global trade slowed down and Jordan's market share gains in garment and chemical exports stagnated.
Foreign investment over the 2003-2009 period averaged US $2.1 billion – 12.9% of GDP – fueled by countries benefiting from the prolonged oil bonanza.
